did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Multi-tree Genetic Programming (MTGP), a machine learning method, for melanoma detection (skin cancer classification) using dermoscopy and standard camera images. It extracts features (LBP, color variation, geometry) and evolves classification models to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) classification approach for melanoma detection. It details specific methods (feature extraction using LBP, color variation, and geometry), defines a new fitness function and genetic operators, and reports empirical results from experiments on two datasets (PH2 and Dermofit) compared against six other classification algorithms.

title: A Multi-tree Genetic Programming Representation for Melanoma Detection Using Local and Global Features
brief_summary: This study develops a novel multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) classification approach for melanoma detection using local and global features from skin cancer images. The method extracts Local Binary Patterns (LBP) for local information and color variation and geometric border shape features for global information, evolving them into separate trees within a single individual. The proposed MTGP method was evaluated on two datasets, PH2 (dermoscopy images) and Dermofit (standard camera images), using binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions. Performance was assessed by comparing the MTGP results against six commonly used classification algorithms and a single-tree GP method over 30 runs. The results demonstrate that the proposed MTGP method significantly outperforms all compared classification methods in terms of accuracy. Additionally, the interpretable nature of the evolved models helps identify prominent features specific to different image capture instruments.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the malignant class in binary classification experiments on both the PH2 dataset (40 instances) and the Dermofit dataset (76 instances).
Used as part of the benign class in the PH2 dataset (80 instances) and as the sole benign class in the Dermofit dataset (331 instances).
Used as part of the benign class in the PH2 dataset (80 instances). The paper notes these are 'currently non-disease lesion, but may develop malignancy later'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign (common nevi, atypical nevi, melanocytic nevus) and malignant (melanoma) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it develops a 'classification approach to melanoma detection' and performs 'binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions' on the PH2 and Dermofit datasets. The fitness function and evaluation metrics are based on classification accuracy (balanced accuracy) between two classes.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing dermoscopy images captured from a specialized instrument (dermatoscope). It consists of 200 images belonging to three classes: common nevi, atypical nevi, and melanomas.

use.training: Used for training the multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) and single-tree GP (STGP) models. The dataset was split into training and test sets, with experiments run 30 times using different random seeds.
use.testing: Used for testing the evolved models to evaluate classification performance (accuracy).

datasets_used.name: Dermofit Image Library
datasets_used.description: A collection of 1300 high-quality skin lesion images captured from a standard camera under standardized conditions with internal color standards. For this study, a subset of two classes was used.

use.training: Used for training the multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) and single-tree GP (STGP) models. The dataset was split into training and test sets, with experiments run 30 times using different random seeds.
use.testing: Used for testing the evolved models to evaluate classification performance (accuracy).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the datasets into training and testing sets for evaluating classification performance.
To compare the performance of the proposed MTGP method against other classification algorithms and determine statistical significance.
To report the results of the 30 independent GP runs, summarizing the central tendency and variability of the accuracy/fitness values.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the ability to discriminate between benign and malignant classes between the proposed MTGP method and the compared classification methods.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the ability to discriminate between benign and malignant classes between the proposed MTGP method and the compared classification methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test was applied to the test results with a significance level of 5%. The symbols '+', '-', and '=' were used to represent significantly better, significantly worse, and not significantly different performance, respectively. The results indicated that the proposed MTGP method significantly outperformed all compared non-GP and single-tree GP methods.

PH2 Dataset.description: Dermoscopy images; 200 total instances (160 benign: 80 common nevi + 80 atypical nevi; 40 malignant: melanoma). Evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation over 30 runs.
PH2 Dataset.notes: MTGP significantly outperformed all non-GP and STGP methods (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < 0.05). Among STGP methods, LBPRGB features showed the highest performance (73.87 ± 2.34).

Dermofit Dataset.description: Standard camera images; 407 total instances (331 benign: Melanocytic Nevus; 76 malignant: Malignant Melanoma). Evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation over 30 runs.
Dermofit Dataset.notes: MTGP significantly outperformed all non-GP and STGP methods (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < 0.05). Among STGP methods, Lesioncolor features showed the highest performance (74.13 ± 2.67).

architecture_details.structure: An individual consisting of four separate trees, each evolved using a different type of feature.

trees.name: Tree 1
trees.input_details: LBP descriptor with window size 3x3 pixels and radius 1 pixel (LBP8,1)

trees.name: Tree 2
trees.features: LBPRGB (177 features: 59 LBP features x 3 color channels)
trees.input_details: LBP descriptor with window size 3x3 pixels and radius 1 pixel (LBP8,1)

trees.name: Tree 3
trees.features: Lesioncolor (12 features: color variation inside lesion and between lesion and skin)

trees.name: Tree 4
trees.features: Lesionshape (11 features: geometrical properties and border information)

genetic_operators.crossover: Same-index-crossover (trees evolve using same feature type only crossover/mutate with each other)

training_details.fitness_function: Average of balanced classification accuracy of all trees in the individual
training_details.training_schedule: Evolutionary process runs for max 50 generations or until perfect individual (100% accuracy) is found. Experiments run 30 times with different random seeds using 10-fold cross-validation.

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: The highest performing tree on the training set is selected and tested on the test data.

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of images to feature vectors using Local Binary Patterns (LBP) for local information extraction from gray and color images.
Extraction of global color variation features (mean and variance of RGB channels, mean ratios) from segmented skin lesion regions.
Extraction of geometry-based features (area, perimeter, greatest diameter, circularity index, irregularity indices, asymmetry indices) from the lesion region.
Use of segmented skin lesion masks for color and geometry feature calculation.
conclusions: The proposed Multi-tree Genetic Programming (MTGP) method significantly outperformed six commonly used classification algorithms (Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Decision Trees, Random Forest, Multilayer Perceptron) and a single-tree GP method in terms of accuracy on both the PH2 and Dermofit datasets.

The MTGP approach effectively integrates local features (Local Binary Patterns from gray and color images) and global features (color variation and geometric border shape) to improve melanoma detection performance compared to methods using single feature types.

The evolved MTGP models are interpretable, allowing dermatologists to identify prominent skin image features specific to different types of skin cancer and different image capture instruments (dermoscopy vs. standard camera), addressing the 'black-box' limitation of deep learning methods like CNNs.

The study confirms that multi-tree GP provides better discriminating ability than single-tree GP across different datasets, as it can automatically evolve a classification model using the most prominent features for the specific image type.

Different feature types contribute differently to classification performance depending on the image source; for instance, on the PH2 dataset (dermoscopy), LBPRGB features were most prominent among STGP methods, while on the Dermofit dataset (standard camera), Lesioncolor features were most prominent among STGP methods.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant), excluding other skin cancer types or multi-class scenarios.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total, with only 40 malignant cases), which may limit the generalizability and statistical power of the results.

The Dermofit dataset used is a subset (407 images) of the full library (1300 images), specifically selecting only Melanocytic Nevus as the benign class, which may not represent the full spectrum of benign lesions.
The method relies on pre-segmented skin lesion masks for feature extraction; performance is contingent on the accuracy of this segmentation step, which is not detailed in the paper.
The proposed MTGP method was evaluated only on two public datasets (PH2 and Dermofit); lack of external validation on independent, multi-center datasets limits clinical applicability assessment.

The paper compares the proposed method against traditional machine learning algorithms (SVM, RF, etc.) and single-tree GP, but does not compare against modern deep learning architectures (e.g., CNNs) which are currently state-of-the-art for this task.
While the method claims interpretability, the complexity of evolved GP trees (depth 2-6) may still be difficult for non-experts to interpret compared to simpler feature importance scores.
The fitness function uses balanced accuracy to handle class imbalance, but the small number of malignant samples in PH2 (40) remains a significant bias risk.

The study does not report sensitivity and specificity separately, only accuracy and balanced accuracy, making it difficult to assess the trade-off between false positives and false negatives in a clinical context.
The computational cost of evolving 1024 individuals for 50 generations over 30 runs is not discussed, which is a practical concern for deployment in resource-constrained settings.
